perturbation.original_text: 2.18 "Patent" or "Patents" means: (a) any and all patents and patent applications owned by Company anywhere in the world as of the Effective Date relating to rare earth mineral processing and rare earth separation including without limitation those patents and patent applications listed in Exhibit A; (b) any and all divisions, continuations, continuations-in-part of any of the patents and patent applications within subdivision (a); (c) any and all patents that may directly or indirectly issue from any patent applications within subdivisions (a) and (b); (d) any and all re- issues, substitutes and extensions of any of the patents within subdivisions (a), (b) and (c); and (e) any and all counterparts or equivalents to any of the foregoing in any country of the world.
perturbation.changed_text: 2.18 "Patent" or "Patents" means: (a) patents and patent applications the Company *chooses* to recognize as of the Effective Date relating to rare earth mineral processing and rare earth separation including only the patents and patent applications listed in Exhibit A; (b) divisions of patents within subdivision (a) *if* the Company approves; (c) patents that *the Company deems* to directly issue from any patent applications within subdivisions (a) and (b); (d) re-issues and extensions of patents within subdivisions (a), (b) and (c) *if the Company consents*; and (e) counterparts or equivalents *if deemed equivalent by the Company* to any of the foregoing in any country of the world.
perturbation.explanation: By adding subjective criteria such as 'chooses to recognize', 'if the Company approves', 'the Company deems' and 'if the Company consents', the definition of 'Patent' is changed. This gives the Company the power to arbitrarily exclude certain intellectual property, undermining the purpose of the agreement and creating uncertainty regarding which patents are actually covered. By modifying the scope, the agreement's ability to ensure IP protection becomes questionable.
perturbation.law_explanation: This modification contradicts 35 U.S.C. § 101, which defines what is patentable subject matter under US law. The contract's revised definition allows the Company to selectively disregard valid patents that would otherwise be protected under the law, essentially deciding what is patentable, superseding the role of the USPTO. The modified contract language introduces uncertainty and directly conflicts with the statute.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 35 U.S. Code § 101 - Inventions patentable | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 35 U.S. Code § 101 - Inventions patentable U.S. Code Notes prev | next Whoever invents or discovers any new and useful process , machine, manufacture, or composition of matter, or any new and useful improvement thereof, may obtain a patent therefor, subject to the conditions and requirements of this title. (July 19, 1952, ch. 950, 66 Stat. 797 .) Historical and Revision Notes Based on Title 35, U.S.C., 1946 ed., § 31 (R.S. 4886, amended (1) Mar. 3, 1897, ch. 391, § 1, 29 Stat. 692 , (2) May 23, 1930, ch. 312, § 1, 46 Stat. 376 , (3) Aug. 5, 1939, ch. 450, § 1, 53 Stat. 1212 ). The corresponding section of existing statute is split into two sections, section 101 relating to the subject matter for which patents may be obtained, and section 102 defining statutory novelty and stating other conditions for patentability. Section 101 follows the wording of the existing statute as to the subject matter for patents, except that reference to plant patents has been omitted for incorporation in section 301 and the word “art” has been replaced by “ process ”, which is defined in section 100. The word “art” in the corresponding section of the existing statute has a different meaning than the same word as used in other places in the statute; it has been interpreted by the courts as being practically synonymous with process or method. “ Process ” has been used as its meaning is more readily grasped than “art” as interpreted, and the definition in section 100(b) makes it clear that “ process or method” is meant. The remainder of the definition clarifies the status of processes or methods which involve merely the new use of a known process , machine, manufacture, composition of matter, or material; they are processes or methods under the statute and may be patented provided the conditions for patentability are sa
perturbation.justification: The modified contract contradicts 35 U.S.C. § 101 by granting the Company the discretion to selectively recognize patents, thereby undermining the statute's definition of patentable subject matter. The original contract objectively defines 'Patent'. The changed contract inserts subjective criteria, like 'chooses to recognize', empowering the Company to decide which patents it will acknowledge. This directly contravenes the legal standard established in 35 U.S.C. § 101, which states that 'Whoever invents or discovers any new and useful process, machine, manufacture, or composition of matter...may obtain a patent therefor'. The changed contract essentially lets the company decide what is patentable, overriding the role of the USPTO as defined by law. This falls under the definition of 'Misaligned Terminology Legal Contradiction' because the contractual language deviates from legally recognized definitions, rendering the contract potentially unenforceable.

perturbation.original_text: 3.00 Company grants to Investor, for the duration of the Option Period, a worldwide, royalty-free, non-exclusive, irrevocable license (with the right to grant sublicenses to Affiliates) under the Patents to practice the methods therein described and claimed and to make and have made, use, offer to sell, sell and import products made using such methods, and to make Improvements, and to engage in any activity which would give rise to a claim of infringement (direct or indirect or otherwise) of one or more of the Patents in the absence of a license.
perturbation.changed_text: 3.00 Company grants to Investor, for the duration of the Option Period, a license (without the right to grant sublicenses) under the Patents to practice the methods therein described and claimed and to make and have made, use, offer to sell, sell and import products made using such methods, and to make Improvements, and to engage in any activity which would give rise to a claim of infringement (direct or indirect or otherwise) of one or more of the Patents in the absence of a license. This license is revocable at the Company's discretion, subject to a penalty of one dollar.
perturbation.explanation: By removing 'worldwide, royalty-free, non-exclusive, irrevocable license (with the right to grant sublicenses to Affiliates)' and introducing 'revocable at the Company's discretion, subject to a penalty of one dollar' the license is changed. This introduces a conflict by turning what was originally intended as strong IP rights, into very easily revocable rights, which fundamentally alters the character and stability of the agreement.
perturbation.contradicted_law: UCC § 2-202. Final Written Expression: Parol or Extrinsic Evidence.
perturbation.law_explanation: This modification introduces parol evidence issues under UCC § 2-202. While the original text implies a comprehensive agreement regarding the license, the added revocation clause introduces ambiguity. If the Investment Agreement, referenced in the IP Agreement, suggests a more robust license, the 'one dollar' revocation clause could be challenged as inconsistent with the parties' intended agreement, creating uncertainty about the enforceability of the license terms.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 2-202. Final Written Expression: Parol or Extrinsic Evidence. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 2-202. Final Written Expression: Parol or Extrinsic Evidence. Terms with respect to which the confirmatory memoranda of the parties agree or which are otherwise set forth in a writing intended by the parties as a final expression of their agreement with respect to such terms as are included therein may not be contradicted by evidence of any prior agreement or of a contemporaneous oral agreement but may be explained or supplemented (a) by course of dealing or usage of trade (Section 1-205 ) or by course of performance (Section 2-208 ); and (b) by evidence of consistent additional terms unless the court finds the writing to have been intended also as a complete and exclusive statement of the terms of the agreement . Uniform Commercial Code Toolbox About Uniform Laws State Uniform Commercial Codes
perturbation.justification: The modification introduces a contradiction under UCC § 2-202 regarding parol evidence. The original text implies a comprehensive agreement, while the added revocation clause with a nominal penalty introduces ambiguity. The 'one dollar' revocation clause could be challenged as inconsistent with the parties' intended agreement, specifically if the investment agreement suggests a more robust license, as per the Law Explanation. Scraped Law Snippet 1, referencing UCC § 2-202, states that 'terms...intended by the parties as a final expression of their agreement...may not be contradicted by evidence of any prior agreement'. The modification effectively allows the company to unilaterally revoke the license for a negligible amount, undermining the originally intended strength and stability of the IP rights granted to the investor, thus creating a contradiction by significantly altering the enforceability of the license terms. The original text implies a more comprehensive license, but the modified text provides a revocable license at the company's discretion, changing the fundamental nature of the agreement.
